reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: CKP/Tanítanék documented commitment to merit-based selection within democratized access (Kockás könyv 2016: 'szelekció nem diszkrimináció'); value derives from belief that talent is broadly distributed and selection mechanisms should not replicate family advantage. The conditional stance reflects alignment with S1's goal of widening access while preserving differentiation.
reactions.interest: Teachers' professional stake in pedagogical coherence and student motivation; equity stake in breaking SES-based pre-sorting; institutional stake in school system legitimacy
reactions.public_good_frame: A selection system that identifies and develops talent across all social groups strengthens both equity and the quality of education; early sorting by SES undercuts both
reactions.argument: S1 aligns with our documented position that selection is legitimate when decoupled from family advantage. We support replacing test-prep-sensitive admission with age-appropriate assessment *if* three conditions hold: (1) the SES indicator is genuinely workable and not gameable—the scenario flags this as unresolved, and we know from practice that address-based or benefit-based proxies are porous and invite migration gaming; (2) non-state maintainers can actually be bound to the same equity rules; the scenario notes this is an 'assumption' with only 'medium confidence', and we have no documented position on the enforceability of subsidy conditionality against church schools in Hungary's legal and political context; (3) the reformed assessment is not simply a relay for a new form of coaching (the scenario itself warns this may happen). We do not have a documented position on whether Hungarian Oktatási Hivatal has the psychometric capacity for a genuinely less-coachable instrument; this is a technical claim we cannot verify. On regional equity quotas *in principle*—yes, this flows from our value that talent is not regionally stratified. But we flag a serious gap: the scenario assumes quotas address the *compositional* problem among applicants but does not reach the non-applicant low-SES families whose absence from the applicant pool is itself the larger equity problem. S1 is a within-applicant reweight, not a demand-side inclusion reform.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move to 'support' if: (a) piloted SES-proxy indicators are validated against administrative registers and shown to be non-gameable; (b) church/foundation maintainers publicly commit to equity quotas under subsidy conditionality, or legislation binds them uniformly; (c) the new assessment is psychometrically validated as resistant to coaching. We would move to 'oppose' if: the SES indicator proves porous and gaming-prone; if non-state maintainers are explicitly exempted; if the reformed assessment becomes another prep-sensitive relay. We would retain 'conditional' if these conditions are partially met or if the design includes a credible demand-side component (information, outreach) to widen the applicant pool itself.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: CKP/Tanítanék documented position (Kockás könyv 2016; Tanítanék javaslatcsomag 2026 draft materials on network rationalization) expresses concern about early sorting but does not take a categorical stance against tracked systems; the position is conditional on whether tracked systems serve equity or entrench it. S2's mechanical cap is consistent with the documented value that 8-12% early sorting is pedagogically and socially excessive, but S2 detaches the cap from admission-rule reform, which we see as a critical gap.
reactions.interest: Teachers' stake in retaining high-achieving students in common schools where they can model and motivate peers; equity stake in preventing permanent SES-based tracking; institutional stake in general-school viability and morale
reactions.public_good_frame: A smaller early-sorted share supports broader peer diversity and reduces the demographic hollowing of general schools, which strengthens motivation and outcomes for the non-selected majority
reactions.argument: We support the goal of reducing early sorting, and we recognize that mechanical caps on entry places are the most direct and durable lever for it. However, S2 has a critical flaw: it proposes to cap entry places *without reforming admission rules*. The scenario itself states that 'absent an S1-type admission-rule change [residual selective places become] even more SES-skewed.' This is not a side-effect; it is mechanical and near-certain. Capping seats from 8–10% to 6–8% of the cohort without changing who is selected among applicants means the remaining places are captured even more exclusively by high-SES families with test prep resources. This is a narrowing, not a democratizing, reform. We also note that S2's equity benefit depends on three unproven assumptions: (1) general schools can absorb and challenge retained high-achievers—the scenario labels this 'the unevidenced retention link'; (2) caps on state schools are not simply offset by non-state maintainers expanding selective intake—the scenario flags this as an 'assumption' with only 'medium confidence'; (3) a successor government does not reverse the caps (Polish precedent). On (1), we know from practice that 'advanced-programme funding' alone does not retain ambitious families if the structural signal is that they are not selected for gifted provision. On (2), we have seen church school expansion in response to state selectivity pressure in recent Hungarian history. On (3), reversal risk is real and serious. S2 without S1 is therefore a narrowing trap that concentrates the harm on low-SES pupils who remain in general schools and lose the high-achieving peers who used to be there. The scenario acknowledges this but does not treat it as a deal-breaker.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move to 'support' if S2 is *bundled with* S1-type admission-rule reform so that the capped selective seats go to a more SES-balanced cohort of applicants. We would move to 'oppose' if non-state maintainers are explicitly exempted from caps or if the cap trajectory is not legislatively entrenched (so reversal becomes cheap and likely). We could accept a 'support' stance if the implementation plan includes a binding commitment that caps apply to non-state maintainers equally and with multi-year legislative entrenchment (e.g. supermajority threshold for reversal), but the scenario flags these as unresolved 'assumptions'. In the current form (cap without admission reform, non-state exemption unresolved), S2 is conditionally acceptable only as a *transitional* measure paired with a time-bound commitment to enact S1 in parallel.

reactions.label: documented
reactions.source: Tanítanék javaslatcsomag (2026 draft, section on 'Komprehenzív alapműködés és késői differenciálás'); CKP public advocacy statements 2023-2025 on delayed selection and teacher professional development
reactions.basis: S3 directly implements the documented CKP/Tanítanék position that late selection (age 14+) is pedagogically and socially superior to early sorting; this position is grounded in Finnish and Polish reform evidence and in the value that teacher autonomy and professional judgment in heterogeneous classrooms is both more equitable and more developmentally sound than age-10 gatekeeping.
reactions.interest: Teachers' professional stake in pedagogical autonomy, classroom heterogeneity, and the ability to differentiate instruction; equity stake in delaying irreversible sorting; institutional stake in the social legitimacy of the teaching profession
reactions.public_good_frame: Delaying selection to age 14 allows diagnosis and differentiation to be based on actual cognitive and motivational development rather than family advantage; teacher professionalism and equity reinforce each other
reactions.argument: S3 is the reform we have documented support for because it aligns selection with genuine pedagogical development and removes the perverse incentive for age-10 test-prep arms races. The evidence base is moderate: cross-country delayed-selection studies (Pekkarinen et al. 2009 on Finland; Jakubowski et al. 2016 on Poland) show that later selection reduces SES-outcome gradients *when bundled with teacher investment and diagnostic support*. S3 is explicit about this bundling, which is essential and which S2 and S4 underestimate. We acknowledge the scenario's honest flagging of four serious risks: (1) the asymmetric peer-effect mechanism is 'contested' in the source literature—we do not overstate this; the mechanism is plausible but not proven for Hungary; (2) sorting may migrate to non-state maintainers and private schools, leaving low-SES pupils in de-tracked general schools with weaker peers—this is the largest implementation risk and the scenario correctly identifies it; (3) teacher supply, especially in maths and science, is acutely constrained—this is real and will require sustained recruitment and retraining investment; (4) reversal after a government change is politically likely (Polish precedent). We do not claim these risks are small. However, we believe they are *manageable* through: sequencing teacher retraining before the pilot (as the implementation steps state), piloting in 2-3 districts with rigorous evaluation before national rollout, attempting a cross-party pact to anchor the reform, and legislating an entrenchment mechanism (supermajority or multi-year notice to reverse). The Polish case shows that reversal is possible but not inevitable if the reform is embedded in institutional commitments and union-party coalitions. We also note that S3 is the only scenario that addresses the *root* problem (early sorting itself) rather than trying to compensate for it (S4) or narrowing it without reforming it (S2). On the migration risk: this is real, but it is *also present in the status quo and S2 and S4*. S3 at least attempts to make general schools attractive to ambitious families by bundling comprehensive-to-14 with genuine investment and diagnostic support, not by hoping that 'advanced programmes' alone will retain them. We are under no illusion that this is easy; we know it requires sustained political commitment and substantial teacher-quality investment. But we believe it is the only reform that addresses equity at the source rather than at the margins.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would remain in 'support' if the implementation plan is modified to: (a) mandate a cross-party pact with a defined fallback (staged, reversible pilot) if the pact fails, rather than a full national mandate without political anchoring; (b) ensure teacher retraining is sequenced *before* and *during* the pilot, not concurrently, and track retraining uptake and classroom-practice change in pilot districts; (c) include a robust registry-based evaluation design that measures sorting migration to non-state maintainers and school choice, with a defined reversal trigger if migration is > 30% (illustrative threshold); (d) legislate a multi-year entrenchment mechanism (e.g. supermajority or 5-year notice for reversal). We would move to 'conditional' if any of these are dropped, particularly if the cross-party pact is not attempted or if the entrenchment mechanism is weak. We would move to 'oppose' if: the teacher-retraining budget is not ring-fenced; if non-state maintainers are explicitly exempted from the comprehensive-to-14 model; if the pilot is not rigorously evaluated before rollout.

reactions.label: no_position
reactions.interest: Teachers' stake in pedagogical quality and student motivation; equity stake in outcomes for disadvantaged pupils
reactions.public_good_frame: High-quality general education, adequately funded and staffed, is a public good whether or not early sorting is retained
reactions.argument: We have no documented position on S4-type compensation packages because CKP/Tanítanék has not published a stance on whether *non-structural* support for general schools is a legitimate alternative to delayed selection. The scenario's premise is that compensation can 'offset, not merely mask' the effects of early sorting—but the scenario itself concedes in its uncertainties that 'no country has demonstrated [this] at Hungary's selection age.' This is honest framing of the pivot on which S4 rests: an unproven assumption. We recognize that the Portuguese TEIP package improved outcomes for disadvantaged schools (moderate evidence), but Portugal did not have early selection at ages 10–12, so the transfer to Hungary is speculative. We also note the scenario's own flagging of a critical political vulnerability: compensation lines are 'the first cut in fiscal consolidation,' which means the gains are reversible and probably temporary. This is not a minor caveat; it goes to the durability of the reform. Finally, we note a tension in the scenario's own reasoning: it proposes to leave the 6- and 8-year gimnázium structure 'unchanged' while simultaneously introducing targeted funding and standards that are *themselves* structural changes to the general-school system. This is not a neutral 'keep the structure' option; it is a reallocation within the structure. We cannot evaluate S4 without clarity on whether it is meant to be a *substitute* for S1 or S3 (in which case our concern about the compensation-vs.-offset gap would be definitive) or a *complement* to them (in which case it is a different question). The scenario frames it as a substitute, and in that form, the unproven assumption (compensation can offset early sorting at Hungary's selection age) is disqualifying for us. We do not oppose targeted support for general schools—that is basic equity investment. But we cannot support S4 as a *replacement* for structural reform, because the core problem (early sorting at age 10–12) is not addressed, only masked. If S4 were framed as a complement to S3 (comprehensive-to-14 *plus* targeted support for the general-school cohort that results), we would have a documented position in its favor, derived from the Finnish bundling evidence that S3 already rests on.
reactions.condition_to_change: Not applicable (no_position). However, we could develop a position on S4 if: (1) the scenario were reframed as a complement to S3 rather than a substitute; (2) a Hungary-specific pilot of S4-type compensation paired with early selection were designed and rigorously evaluated, with a pre-specified threshold for whether compensation actually offsets sorting effects (not just improves absolute outcomes); (3) the compensation budget were legislatively ring-fenced with a multi-year protection mechanism, not subject to fiscal-cycle cuts. Without these conditions, S4 remains an untested hypothesis that we cannot endorse.
